\section*{Abstract}
{\bf 
    We uncover an effective and communicative set of agents working with \mg. Agentic installation, learning-by-doing training, and user support provide easy access to state-of-the-art simulations and accelerate LHC research. We show in detail how \ma interact with inexperienced and advanced users, support a range of simulation tasks, and analyze results. In a second step, we illustrate how \ma automatize event generation and run an autonomous simulation campaign, starting from a pdf file of a paper.
}

{
\vspace{5pt}
\noindent \url{https://github.com/heidelberg-hepml/MadAgents}
}

\section{Introduction}

The challenge of the current and future LHC program is how to analyze vast amounts of complex data in terms of fundamental physics, specifically quantum field theory. This precision-LHC program rests on the comparison of measured scattering events with first-principle predictions provided by multi-purpose event generators, such as  \pythia~\cite{Sjostrand:2014zea}, \mg~\cite{Maltoni:2002qb,Alwall:2007st,Alwall:2011uj,Alwall:2014hca}, \sherpa~\cite{Sherpa:2019gpd}, and \herwig~\cite{Bewick:2023tfi}. Modern machine learning (ML)~\cite{Butter:2022rso,Plehn:2022ftl} allows us to benefit from the sizes of the observed and simulated datasets, so that we can extract all available information despite the complexity. Crucially, this acceleration has to cover the efficiency of simulation tools and of analysis workflows.

If we want to understand the LHC dataset in terms of quantum field theory, ML-enhanced event generators must be faster and more precise. This holds for new physics searches and for precision analyses, unified by the question: \textsl{Which Lagrangian describes the LHC data?} Specifically for \mg,  \madnis~\cite{Heimel:2022wyj,Heimel:2023ngj,Heimel:2024wph} sampling and uncertainty-aware amplitude surrogates~\cite{Bahl:2024gyt,Bahl:2025xvx,Bahl:2026qaf} provide a comprehensive ML basis to accelerate it to the point where, for instance, higher orders in perturbation theory and high-multiplicity final states are available at minimal additional computational cost. Similar ML improvements are on the way for \sherpa~\cite{janssen:2025zke,Herrmann:2025nnz}, \pythia~\cite{Butter:2025wxn}, and \herwig~\cite{Chan:2023icm}, forming a foundation of highly efficient computational tools for the HL-LHC program.

Given the availability of accelerated theory simulation tools, we now have to ensure that these tools can be used efficiently. Specifically, the next-generation computational structure of \mg7 has to be accompanied by ML-accelerated user training and support. This starts with the installation and includes learning-by-doing \mg runs for inexperienced users or incoming graduate students, as well as support for advanced users. The state of the art for such a support are physics chatbots, based on large language models (LLMs), often organized as agents. In this paper, we present \ma as a physics-specific agentic \mg support tool.

However, ML agents not only make established research or analysis tasks more convenient, they can accelerate them to the point where  ML agents transform the way we do research. It has already been illustrated how agents can perform, essentially, an entire experimental LHC analysis~\cite{Diefenbacher:2025zzn} or a theoretical particle physics calculation~\cite{Schwartz:2026ekw}. Following this path, we will show how \ma can run autonomous simulation campaigns, extracting the relevant information from given articles and from available web content. Such autonomous event generation will expand agentic analysis methods towards theory-based precision measurements and global LHC analyses.

This paper is not intended to provide new physics insights, but it changes the way we do LHC event generation. It combines the precision simulation capabilities of current and future \mg releases with the ease of the web-based \mg4~\cite{Alwall:2007st}.  In Sec.~\ref{sec:ma} we introduce the structure and implementation of \ma. In Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_install} we show how \ma install not only \mg, but also related codes such as \delphes and even \textsc{\root}. Next, in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_train} we illustrate how \ma help inexperienced users by designing dedicated \mg tutorials, running simulations, and performing standard tasks such as event reweighting. In Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_adv} we turn to the support of experienced users, for instance, the simulation of top pair production beyond leading-order on-shell precision and understanding the underlying theory assumptions. Finally, in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_auto} we illustrate how \ma run automatized simulation campaigns, reproducing a simulation from a publication without any further communication with the user. In the appendices we will give many more details, all of which are also available together with our public code base.

\clearpage
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Setup}
\label{sec:ma}

Large Language Models (LLMs)~\cite{zhao2023survey} are autoregressive, generative networks based on the transformer architecture. For language generation, a text is first encoded in a sequence of discrete tokens. The set of tokens is finite and typically has around $10^5$ elements. The transformer encodes a categorical distribution over the token space conditioned on a variable-length sequence of tokens. To generate text, we sample a token from this distribution and append it to an existing sequence. This is repeated until we sample a stop token. State-of-the-art LLMs are able to input multi-modal data~\cite{yin2024survey}, most commonly images. Those are first preprocessed with an additional network, typically a vision transformer, into image tokens which can be interleaved with other tokens.

LLMs can invoke tools~\cite{parisi2022talm, schick2023toolformer} as predefined functions, for which the LLM generates the arguments. This way, the LLM queries information and interacts with its environment. The latter is commonly implemented as a React loop~\cite{yao2022react}, where an LLM searches for a way to accomplish a user's goal, executes a tool, digests its output, and repeats this loop until the goal is fulfilled. Since tool calls and output are part of the LLM's context, it can correct errors~\cite{chen2023teaching} and teach itself to write and use software~\cite{wang2023voyager, cai2023large, yang2024swe}. LLMs perform better if they first output a reasoning sequence, the chain-of-thought, and only then provide the answer or call a tool~\cite{wei2022chain}. For some LLMs, the reasoning effort is configurable. 

LLMs can be instructed to specialize on specific tasks through a system prompt. It is prepended to the conversation and defines workflow, constraints, answering styles, etc. A set of task-specialized LLMs, called agents, have shown to outperform one general-purpose LLM~\cite{du2023improving, liu2023dynamic, wang2024survey}. To manage the agents, an orchestrating agent usually delegates task to specialized agents~\cite{hong2023metagpt, chen2024survey}. We will use this structure to support \mg users through \ma.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection{LLMs for fundamental physics}

LLMs for physics research have a rich history dominated by astrophysical and cosmological applications. A first obvious LLM task is literature search. astroBERT~\cite{grezes2021buildingastrobertlanguagemodel} is the first LLM trained on 400,000 ADS papers, but without generative capabilities.  AstroLLaMA~\cite{Nguyen:2023nhp} is a generative LLaMA-2, fine-tuned on 300,000 cosmological arXiv abstracts and able to generate new abstracts. AstroLLaMa-Chat~\cite{UniverseTBD:2024pmh}, adds introductions and conclusions to the AstroLLaMA training dataset, allowing it to answer physics questions. The AstroMLab study~\cite{ting2024astromlab1winsastronomy} then compares a range of astronomical literature LLMs and introduces a benchmarking dataset with 4425 multiple-choice questions. An interesting question is how the commercial Claude LLMs compare to domain-specific solutions. Cosmosage~\cite{deHaan:2024ybs} starts from the public Mistral model and includes question-answer tuning. It is fine-tuned on astrophysical texts. In particle physics, FeynTune~\cite{Richmond:2025lzg} allows for arXiv searches including hep-ph, hep-th, and gr-qc abstracts. It uses standard  LoRa fine-tuning on Llama~3.1.

From a scientific and agent perspective, Mephisto~\cite{sun2025interpretingmultibandgalaxyobservations} is much more exciting, as it defines an agentic analysis of multi-band galaxy observations. It works with the latest James Webb data, in terms of spectral energy distributions, and includes Little Red Dot galaxies as a current research question. The LLM interacts with the CIGALE code base, develops the best parameter setting, and mimics scientific intuition about these parameters.  The CMBAgent~\cite{Laverick:2024fyh} works on Atacama Cosmology Telescope data with its public likelihood exists. It relies on the CLASS tool set to build a simulation and inference pipeline. 
%The paper uses Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) rather than fine-tuning of LLMs,
It combines Retrieval Augmented Generation agents, coder agents, and manager agents, but relies on five successive steps with human feedback. Its result is a CMB inference for instance of $H_0$, $\sigma_8$, and $\Omega_c h^2$. The AI Cosmologist~\cite{Moss:2025ynt} combines planning, coding, execution, analysis, synthesis, and literature agents. Its output includes a research paper. Finally, SimAgents~\cite{Zhang:2025uoo} focuses on setting up simulations and processing the simulated data using a physics and a simulation agent. The simulations are checked using a visualization and a simple statistical analysis. It serves as a key inspiration for our \ma. Finally, L3M~\cite{Heneka:2025fpe} serves as a first example of how LLMs can be fine-tuned to process physics data with the help of simple connector networks.

The first particle physics agents are being developed right now. Experimental Agents for Discovery~\cite{Diefenbacher:2025zzn} automatize an anomaly search with the help of a researcher, a coder, a code reviewer, and a logic reviewer agent. The paper discusses in detail the prompting of an autonomous analysis agent. ArgoLOOM~\cite{Bakshi:2025fgx} combines particle physics, nuclear physics, and cosmology. On the particle physics side it runs \mg through an OpenAI API, where the UFO file allows it to go beyond particle physics. The Berkeley analysis agent~\cite{Gendreau-Distler:2025fsj} maximizes the expected significance for $H \to \gamma \gamma$ given simulated datasets. It focuses on standard experimental tasks, converting \root files and separating signal and background using a TabPFN transformer, and turning the outcome into a statistical statement. The paper compares different LLMs for each steps. Finally, HEPTAPOD~\cite{Menzo:2025cim} is a particle physics simulation agent that does not attempt to be autonomous. Given a model, it generates events using \mg and \pythia, reconstructs jets, and searches for resonances. 

For cosmological simulation tools it is becoming standard to provide LLM agents to help set up, run, and analyze the simulations, for instance, for CLASS/\href{https://github.com/santiagocasas/clapp/}{CLAP} and \href{https://github.com/franciscovillaescusa/CAMELS_Agents/}{CAMEL}. In particle physics, such simulation support agents do not exist yet.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection{Structure}

%-----------------------------------------
\begin{figure}[b!]
  \centering
  \input{figs/madagants_overview_tikz.tex}
  \caption{\ma structure.}
  \label{fig:madagents-overview}
\end{figure}
%-----------------------------------------

The agentic structure of \ma is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:madagents-overview}. It is built with \href{https://www.langchain.com/langgraph}{LangGraph} and uses the established orchestrating architecture. Instead of a strict sequence of agent actions, an Orchestrator decides the chain of agent calls. It chooses whether to message the user or to instruct a specific agent. For the latter, it decides which agent to call and generates an instruction. The selected agent carries out the instructed task and reports back to the Orchestrator. This allows \ma to adapt their response to the task and gives the user access to its workflow through instructions. We distinguish two main agent types:
%
\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Controlling agents} manage and control the whole workflow, observe the user messages and the Orchestrator messages with the associated agent responses. The Orchestrator delegates straight-forward tasks directly to a Worker and otherwise instructs the Planner to generate a multi-step plan. For such a plan, the Orchestrator works on one plan step at a time and keeps the plan updated by invoking the Plan-Updater. Finally, the Orchestrator asks the Reviewer for its judgment after creating plans,  after worker agents produce user-facing results, after steps with expensive failure modes and before reporting final outcomes to the user. Depending on the Reviewer's response, the Orchestrator might decide to revise the step or the plan.
  \begin{itemize}
  \item Orchestrator fulfills the user request by invoking the specialized workers with suitable instructions; It interacts with the user, clarifies open or ambiguous user requests, delegates tasks to the specialized worker agents, and reports final results and answers. While the Orchestrator can in principle control the whole workflow, we support it with three specialized controlling agents;
  \item Planner generates a multi-step plan for solving a complex user request;
  \item Plan-Updater, a light-weight agent that only sees the Orchestrator instructions and translates them into updates of a given plan. 
  \item Reviewer verifies the worker results and the progress of the user request.
  \end{itemize}
\item \textbf{Worker agents} carry out requested tasks and only see a restricted context. Each Worker is conditioned only on their work, such that it sees the Orchestrator instructions, their internal workflows including tool calls, and their final replies to the Orchestrator.
  \begin{itemize}
  \item MG-Operator  specializes in the \mg software. We explicitly include an instruction for common use-patterns of \mg into its system prompt. It can modify and inspect the file system, run bash scripts, and access a dedicated interactive command line interface (CLI);
  \item Script-Operator specializes in bash and \python. Similarly to the MG-operator, its capabilities include modifying and inspecting the file system and running bash scripts. It should be used for CLI tasks that do not require using the \mg software;
  \item CLI-Operator accesses the user's interactive CLI. It can inspect the current state of the CLI and execute commands on user request;
  \item Plotter creates figures, following a default guidance, \eg use LaTeX for mathematical symbols, unless the user explicitly overwrites it in the prompt. Furthermore, it is instructed to iteratively inspect every figure and adjust if visibility is bad or plot requirements are not fulfilled;
  \item PDF-Reader extracts information from a pdf-file and can inspect references by searching the web;
  \item Researcher extracts information from the web.
  \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}
%    
The MG-Operator, the Script-Operator, the CLI-Operator, and the Plotter can search the web in case they do not know how to proceed or they cannot solve an error. For long context histories, we resort to the standard approach of summarizing the oldest message history with a Summarizer agent. We append this summary to the corresponding agent's system prompts. Details of this agent system are provided in App.~\ref{app:agentic_details}.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection{Implementation}

Our agentic support system $(i)$ launches a containerized environment; $(ii)$ launches a local back-end and front-end server hosting \ma and enables web-browser communication; $(iii)$ launches an interactive command line session inside the container; and $(iv)$ attaches the user's command line terminal and \ma to this command line session. The user can work within the container without having to communicate with \ma. Once the user decides to do so, \ma can be invoked via the web-browser. The container, built and run via \href{https://apptainer.org/}{Apptainer}, hosts all applications, in particular \ma and \mg. We provide two pre-defined container setups:
%
\begin{enumerate}
\item A basic \mg installation containing \fastjet, \lhapdf, \pythia8, \delphes, and a basic set of \python packages. For standard \mg workflows, this image can be used for plug-and-play solutions.
\item Leaving out any \mg or \python related installations. This container can be used for version-specific installations which \ma can create.
\end{enumerate}
%
The directory structure includes:
%
\begin{itemize}
\item \lstinline|/output|: bind-mounted from the host file system to the container for the user work. \ma will save user-requested output there.
\item \lstinline|/runs/workdirs|: bind-mounted from the host file system to the container to store \ma internal data. A custom directory is assigned to each run, inside which the \ma workspace folder is located. The workspace, system-linked to \lstinline|/workspace|, is a directory dedicated to \ma for temporary and non-user-facing files.
\item \lstinline|/runs/pdf_files|: bind-mounted from the host file system to the container and system-linked to \lstinline|/pdf_files|, for users to place pdf files. Inside the container, this directory is read-only.
\item \lstinline|/opt|: to place software installations.
\end{itemize}
%
Any directory that is not bind-mounted to the host will still keep its contents between container runs. Apptainer stores those changes in the container’s overlay filesystem, so installs remain available across \ma invocations. 

\clearpage
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Agentic \mg}
\label{sec:apps}

There are several ways for \ma to improve and accelerate LHC research, from installing \mg and related software (including \root) in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_install} to training-level support in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_train}, experienced user support in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_adv} and autonomous event generation in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_auto}. Throughout our examples, we use \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5.1}{GPT-5.1} for all agents except the Plan-Updater, which uses \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5-mini}{GPT-5 mini}. We remind the reader that LLM responses are not deterministic. As \mg and the associated tools are open-source, basic instructions and basic knowledge are encoded in the trained LLM. This allows \ma to find and implement many plans and solutions without external information.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection{Software Installation}
\label{sec:apps_install}

As a first step, we show how \ma can install \mg and associated public codes autonomously. We deliberately make this task hard by asking \ma to compile \root instead of downloading pre-compiled binaries. For this first example, we describe the \ma workflow in detail, including the plan creation and review steps. We start with the clean container environment and query,
%
\begin{userblock}[User]
Please install the latest stable release of MadGraph together with Pythia8 and Delphes under \lstinline|/opt|:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Install MadGraph under \lstinline|/opt/MG5_aMC| so that the main executable is located inside the directory \lstinline|/opt/MG5_aMC/bin|.
    \item Install Python dependencies into the MAD environment.
    \item Only install dependencies that are strictly required for a successful installation (i.e., missing them causes the install/build to fail). Install any such missing dependencies under \lstinline|/opt|. Don’t ask for additional details about dependency installation decisions; use your best judgment and proceed.\\
    If ROOT is required, build and install ROOT from source (compile it yourself) rather than using any precompiled/prebuilt ROOT binaries.
\end{itemize}

These installations must persist for future sessions.

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\textbf{Prompt explanation:} We tell \ma that this installation is not temporary and must persist for other \ma runs. This forces \ma to modify the persistent directories, for which \ma requires explicit permission. To skip this question, we specify the exact installation location. Furthermore, we give \ma direct permission to install dependencies, which otherwise might result in \ma asking follow-up questions.
\end{userblock}
%
For this complex task, \ma decides to create a plan which the reviewer checks and accepts,
%
\begin{planblock}[Plan]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*, label=\textbf{Step \arabic*:}, itemsep=0.6em]
\item Inspect existing environment and prerequisites
\item Download and install the latest stable MadGraph version
\item Install minimal required Python dependencies for MadGraph
\item Build and install ROOT from source
\item Install and integrate Pythia8 with MadGraph
\item Install and integrate Delphes with MadGraph
\item Finalize configuration, document paths, and run validation tests
\end{enumerate}
\end{planblock}
%
First, the Script-Operator inspects the container environment and \ma mark the first step as finished through the output
%
\begin{planblock}[\ma outcome of Step 1]
Environment under /opt inspected. Findings: /opt/MG5\_aMC does not exist; there is no existing MadGraph, Pythia, Delphes, or ROOT installation under /opt. /opt/envs/MAD exists and is a functional Python 3.11.2 environment with pip available. /opt/root does not exist. Core build tools gcc, g++, gfortran, make, cmake, git, tar, and gzip are all present in the system PATH. No filesystem modifications were made.
\end{planblock}
%
In the next two steps, \ma install \mg. The MG-Operator downloads and installs the \mg binaries. After the reviewer verifies the success, \ma report
%
\begin{planblock}[\ma outcome of Step 2]
MadGraph5\_aMC installed under /opt/MG5\_aMC. Version file reports version = 3.7.0, date = 2026-01-05. Standard MG5 tree present (aloha, madgraph, models, Template, bin, VERSION, etc.). /opt/MG5\_aMC/bin/mg5\_aMC exists, is a Python script with \#!/usr/bin/env python3 shebang, and has executable permissions. No extra system or Python packages were installed in this step. Basic invocation of mg5\_aMC using the /opt/envs/MAD Python 3.11.2 environment fails only due to missing Python module six; no other installation or permission errors observed. Persistent files confined to /opt/MG5\_aMC; temporary artifacts under /workspace/mg5\_src.
\end{planblock}
%
The Script-Operator installs the missing dependencies. Once \mg can start, the reviewer judges the outcome and \ma note
%
\begin{planblock}[\ma outcome of Step 3]
Configured /opt/envs/MAD (Python 3.11.2, pip 25.3) so that MadGraph5\_aMC runs without Python import errors. Using /opt/envs/MAD/bin/python to run /opt/MG5\_aMC/bin/mg5\_aMC initially failed due to missing module six. Installed only the required package six via '/opt/envs/MAD/bin/python -m pip install six' (six==1.17.0 in /opt/envs/MAD/lib/python3.11/site-packages). After installation, MG5 3.7.0 starts under /opt/envs/MAD and executes simple command files (e.g. set automatic\_html\_opening False; quit) without any ImportError or ModuleNotFoundError. Remaining messages are only warnings about external tools (fastjet-config, eMELA-config, lhapdf-config, EPS viewer, browser), not Python dependency issues. No other Python packages were added in this step.
\end{planblock}
%
Now, \ma target the \root installation as the most challenging step. Mentioning \root in the prompt reminds \ma that \root is required by \delphes. If we omit the explicit \root instruction, \ma will most likely not have a planned step to install \root, encounter an error while installing \delphes, trace the error to a missing \root installation, and install it then. 

\ma start by inspecting the operating system and the installed compilers. They download  \root from the official source to \lstinline|/workspace/root_build|, unpack it, create a build directory, configure a minimal \root build for \delphes, and compile it with parallel jobs. After a few minutes, the bash execution is sent to the background and its \lstinline|stderr| and \lstinline|stdout| streams are forwarded to files. This allows \ma to call tools in parallel. \ma track the compilation by periodically checking the bash output and putting themselves to sleep for a few minutes. After successful compilation, they install \root on \lstinline|/opt/root| and verify the installation by inspecting the \root directory and executing \lstinline|root-config|. This installation is judged by the reviewer, and \ma summarize\footnote{Throughout the paper we use [\dots] to indicate a shortened \ma prompt or output} 
%
\begin{planblock}[\ma outcome of Step 4 (shortened)]
Built and installed ROOT 6.30.06 from the official source tarball (\url{https://root.cern/download/root_v6.30.06.source.tar.gz}) using CMake and make. [\ldots]\ thisroot.sh and related env scripts are present in /opt/root/bin. No prebuilt ROOT binaries or external ROOT packages were used; ROOT was built entirely from source and installed under /opt/root without adding extra external dependencies beyond ROOT’s builtin third-party components.
\end{planblock}
%
Next, the MG-Operator installs \pythia and \delphes, each using the \mg installation function. Both installations are verified by the reviewer, and \ma note
%
\begin{planblock}[\ma outcome of Step 5 (shortened)]
Installed and integrated Pythia8 with MadGraph5\_aMC under /opt using MG5’s internal HEPTools installer. Ran MG5 in batch mode via /opt/envs/MAD to execute 'install pythia8', which downloaded and built Pythia8 and related HEPTools under |/opt/MG5\_aMC/HEPTools. [\ldots] \ 
MG5aMC\_PY8\_interface was installed under /opt/MG5\_aMC/HEPTools/MG5aMC\_PY8\_interface. [\ldots] A test MG5 run [\ldots] executed via /opt/envs/MAD completed successfully: [\ldots]
\end{planblock}
%
and
%
\begin{planblock}[\ma outcome of Step 6 (shortened)] 
Installed and integrated Delphes with MadGraph5\_aMC using ROOT from /opt/root. Initial MG5 'install Delphes' failed due to MG5 requiring a 'root' executable on PATH; our ROOT build under /opt/root/bin provided 'root.exe' and 'root-config' but no 'root'. Created a minimal symlink /opt/root/bin/root -> root.exe to satisfy MG5's check. [\ldots] re-ran 'install Delphes' via MG5's installer under /opt/MG5\_aMC. Delphes was built successfully and installed at /opt/MG5\_aMC/Delphes [\ldots]  A functional MG5 test chain [\ldots] run via /opt/envs/MAD with the ROOT environment set as above, completed successfully: [\ldots]
\end{planblock}
%
\ma now finalize the installation with the Script-Operator. As this step is straight-forward, it does not require an additional reviewer invocation and \ma marks the final step as accomplished with the outcome
%
\begin{planblock}[\ma outcome of Step 7 (shortened)]
Final validation and summarization completed. Confirmed that /opt/MG5\_aMC/bin/mg5\_aMC exists, is executable, and runs cleanly under the /opt/envs/MAD\ Python environment for a trivial command file [\ldots], with only expected external-tool warnings and no Python import errors. Collected version information: MadGraph5\_aMC version 3.7.0 (from /opt/MG5\_aMC/VERSION, date 2026-01-05); ROOT version 6.30.06 (from \/opt/root/bin/root-config --version); Pythia8 version 8.316 (from /opt/MG5\_aMC/HEPTools/pythia8/bin/pythia8-config --version); Delphes version 3.5.0 (from \/opt/MG5\_aMC/Delphes/CHANGELOG). Verified key installation paths: [\ldots] Confirmed these directories exist and have expected top-level contents. [\ldots]
\end{planblock}
%
Before reporting back to the user, \ma ask the reviewer to judge whether the user goal has been achieved completely. After the reviewer confirms this, \ma reports
%
\begin{finalblock}[\ma (shortened)]
Here is what is now installed and how to use it.

\textbf{1. MadGraph5\_aMC}

\begin{itemize}
\item Install location: \lstinline|/opt/MG5_aMC|
\item Version: 3.7.0 [...]
\item Main executable: \lstinline|/opt/MG5_aMC/bin/mg5_aMC| [...]
\item {[...]}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{How to run MG5}

Use the MAD environment’s Python explicitly, e.g.

\begin{bashblock}
cd /opt/MG5_aMC
PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 /opt/envs/MAD/bin/python /opt/MG5_aMC/bin/mg5_aMC
\end{bashblock}
[...]

\textbf{2. Python environment (\lstinline|/opt/envs/MAD|)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Path: \lstinline|/opt/envs/MAD|
\item Python: 3.11.2
\item {[...]}
\item Only MG5-specific addition: \lstinline|six|
\end{itemize}

\textbf{3. ROOT (for Delphes)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Built \textbf{from source} (no prebuilt binaries) from: \url{https://root.cern/download/root_v6.30.06.source.tar.gz}
\item Install prefix: \lstinline|/opt/root|
\item Version: 6.30.06
\item {[...]}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Typical ROOT environment setup (if you need ROOT directly)}

[...]
\begin{bashblock}
source /opt/root/bin/thisroot.sh
\end{bashblock}
[...]

\textbf{4. Pythia8}

\begin{itemize}
\item Installed using MG5’s \lstinline|install pythia8| [...]
\item Version: 8.316 [...]
\item Location: \lstinline|/opt/MG5_aMC/HEPTools/pythia8| [...]
\item {[...]}
\item Additional HEPTools components built and kept under \lstinline|/opt/MG5_aMC/HEPTools/| (by MG5’s installer): HEPMC, LHAPDF (Python 3), zlib, etc.
\end{itemize}
[...]

\textbf{5. Delphes}

\begin{itemize}
\item Installed via MG5’s \lstinline|install Delphes| [...]
\item Location: \lstinline|/opt/MG5_aMC/Delphes| [...]
\item Version: 3.5.0. [...]
\item {[...]}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Test performed (MG5 + Pythia8 + Delphes)}

MG5 command file (schematically):

\begin{bashblock}
set automatic_html_opening False
import model sm
generate e+ e- > mu+ mu-
output /workspace/mg5_test_delphes_output
launch /workspace/mg5_test_delphes_output
shower=PYTHIA8
detector=Delphes
0
quit
\end{bashblock}
[...]

Result: parton-level generation, Pythia8 showers, and the Delphes step all completed without ROOT/Delphes errors (10k events, consistent cross section). [...]
\end{finalblock}

\clearpage
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection{Supporting and training inexperienced users}
\label{sec:apps_train}

Our second \ma application is not an autonomous task, but training and support of inexperienced users. Here, it is crucial that the \ma communicate what they are doing, where problems appear, and how they solve these problems. We provide three levels of support, from tailored tutorials in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_train_tut} to learning-by-doing in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_train_learn} and comprehensive documentation of more involved tasks in Sec.~\ref{sec:apps_train_doc}.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsubsection{Tailored tutorials}
\label{sec:apps_train_tut}

We begin by asking \ma to train an inexperienced user interactively and through tutorials tailored to their research tasks,

\begin{userblock}[User]
I am a physics PhD student and completely new to MadGraph. My task is to generate LHC events for BSM models (expressed as an EFT) and then compare the generated events to real data via SBI.

Be my MadGraph teacher. Please create tutorials in \lstinline|/output/tutorials| where I learn the basics of generating the relevant data via MadGraph (my supervisor told me that Pythia and Delphes suffice for the beginning). I should solve the tutorials in \lstinline|/output/tutorials/my_work/<dedicated_tutorial_folder>|. I will inspect the tutorials with VS Code and work through them via my interactive CLI that you have access to as well.

When creating the tutorials: Do not save or state the solutions to the tutorials anywhere, not even inside the worker's execution trace.

In your reply, include an overview of the tutorials you created: for each tutorial, give a short description and explain what I should learn from it.

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\textbf{Prompt explanation:} We inform \ma of our situation, our current knowledge and the future task that it must prepare us for. We define \ma's teaching role, specify which directories it should modify and explicitly mention that we will work through the tutorials in the container environment. This allows \ma to not only tailor the tutorials on our research task, but also on the environment. Finally, we instruct \ma to not disclose any solution at this stage and to include an overview of the created tutorials.
\end{userblock}
%
\ma develop a plan and work through it to create the tutorials
%
\begin{planblock}[Plan]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*, label=\textbf{Step \arabic*:}, itemsep=0.6em]
\item \textbf{Design tutorial sequence and structure:}\\
\textbf{Outcome:} Designed a sequence of 6 tutorials with folder names, titles, prerequisites, learning objectives, and open-ended exercise prompts; saved detailed design in /workspace/madgraph\_tutorials/tutorial\_design\_overview.md.\\
\textbf{Agents:} Script-Operator

\item \textbf{Create tutorial tree for tutorials and user work:}\\ 
\textbf{Outcome:} Created /output/tutorials with six tutorial subdirectories and a top-level index README, plus mirrored work directories under /output/tutorials/my\_work with minimal README placeholders; no solutions or numeric answers were added.\\
\textbf{Agents:} Script-Operator

\item \textbf{Write introductory MG5/Pythia/Delphes tutorials (basics and SM processes):}\\ 
\textbf{Outcome:} Created detailed beginner-friendly README tutorials in /output/tutorials/01\_mg5\_basics and /output/tutorials/02\_sm\_drell\_yan\_parton covering MG5 basics, the MG5→Pythia→Delphes workflow, and SM Drell–Yan at parton level with open-ended exercises and no solutions or numeric answers.\\
\textbf{Agents:} MG-Operator $\rightarrow$ Reviewer

\item \textbf{Write tutorials on Pythia showering and Delphes detector simulation:}\\ 
\textbf{Outcome:} Created detailed tutorials in /output/tutorials/03\_pythia\_showering and /output/tutorials/04\_delphes\_detector covering Pythia showering and Delphes detector simulation, with open-ended exercises and no solutions or numeric answers.\\
\textbf{Agents:} MG-Operator $\rightarrow$ Reviewer

\item \textbf{Write tutorials on BSM EFT models and SBI-oriented outputs:}\\ 
\textbf{Outcome:} Created detailed tutorials in /output/tutorials/05\_bsm\_eft\_models and /output/tutorials/06\_sbi\_dataset\_preparation covering EFT UFO models in MG5, EFT parameter scans, and SBI-oriented dataset and metadata design, with open-ended exercises and no solutions or numeric answers.\\
\textbf{Agents:} MG-Operator $\rightarrow$ Reviewer

\item \textbf{Consistency and quality review, then provide user-facing overview:}\\ 
\textbf{Outcome:} Performed a light automated consistency scan over all six tutorial README files to check for structural issues (forbidden wording, correct my\_work references, path sanity). No problematic numeric answers or explicit solutions were detected under these checks. Created a high-level, solution-free overview of all tutorials at /workspace/madgraph\_tutorials/tutorials\_overview\_for\_user.md, summarizing titles, content, and learning outcomes.\\
\textbf{Agents:} Script-Operator
\end{enumerate}
\end{planblock}
%
After verifying that the user goal is completed, \ma replies
%
\begin{promptblock}[\ma (shortened)]
I’ve created a 6-part tutorial suite under \lstinline|/output/tutorials|, with matching work areas under \lstinline|/output/tutorials/my_work|. Below is an overview of each tutorial: what it’s called, where it lives, and what you’ll learn. [...]

\begin{itemize}
 \item \textbf{What it covers:}
 \begin{itemize}

 \item The overall workflow: MG5 (matrix elements, parton level) $\to$ Pythia (showers, hadronization) $\to$ Delphes (detector level).
 \item Installing/obtaining MG5\_aMC (at a generic, OS-agnostic level) and launching the \lstinline|mg5_aMC| interface.
 \item Basic MG5 commands (\lstinline|import model|, \lstinline|generate|, \lstinline|output|, \lstinline|launch|, \lstinline|display ...|) and the structure of a process directory (\lstinline|Cards/|, \lstinline|Events/|, etc.).
  \end{itemize}

\item Defining Drell–Yan–like processes in MG5 using the Standard Model and multiparticle labels for protons and leptons.
\item Using \lstinline|run_card.dat| (beams, PDFs, cuts, number of events, etc.) and \lstinline|param_card.dat| (SM parameters) in a concrete example.

\textbf{Tutorial 3 – Pythia: from partons to hadrons}
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{What it covers:}
\begin{itemize}
\item The roles of initial-state radiation, final-state radiation, hadronization, and underlying event in Pythia.
\item How to start from an MG5 process directory with LHE events (e.g. Drell–Yan) and enable Pythia showering via the MG5 run interface.
\item Where Pythia configuration lives (shower card, tunable settings, seeds) in a typical MG5 setup.

\vspace{1em}

\textbf{Tutorial 6 – Preparing datasets for SBI}
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{What it covers:}

\item Designing a directory structure for simulations over parameter points: [...]
\item Designing metadata [...]
\item Conceptual discussion of event-level vs.\ summary-statistic representations and how they might be used for SBI (without implementing any SBI algorithms).
\item Strategies for train/validation/test splits in parameter space and for ensuring reproducibility.
\end{itemize}

\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\textbf{Where to look and how to proceed}
\begin{itemize}
