\end{itemize}

A good way to get started is to open \lstinline|/output/tutorials| in VS Code, read through \lstinline|01_mg5_basics/README.md|, and then begin working in \lstinline|my_work/01_mg5_basics| from your CLI. When you’re comfortable with MG5 basics and parton-level Drell–Yan, continue in order through Pythia, Delphes, EFT, and finally the SBI dataset design.

If you’d like, I can next help you plan how to tackle these tutorials over time (e.g. which ones to do before you start touching real data or SBI code).
\end{promptblock}
%
\ma accompany the user through the \href{https://github.com/heidelberg-hepml/MadAgents/tree/main/supplementary/tutorials}{tutorials}, provide detailed explanations and hints, and verify solutions. As an example, we work through the 5th tutorial, shown in App~\ref{app:ex_tutorial}. The first exercise requires loading a UFO model and inspecting it in \mg. After looking at the available UFO models in \mg, we ask \ma for help choosing a model
%
\begin{userblock}[User]
I skip tutorials 1–4 and go directly to tutorial 5. I see many UFO models and I don’t know which one to load for this tutorial. Please help me.
\end{userblock}
%
\ma reads the output of the user's CLI and responds
%
\begin{promptblock}[\ma (shortened)]
There is no single ``correct'' UFO for Tutorial 5 -- the tutorial is about \textit{how} to work with an EFT UFO, not about a specific physics choice. But given the models you have available, here is a pragmatic way to proceed.

\textbf{1. Quick sanity check with your supervisor}

If your project already has a specific EFT in mind (e.g.\ a particular top EFT, VBS EFT, DM EFT, etc.), the safest is to ask your supervisor which UFO name to use. That will align your tutorial work with your actual analysis.

\textbf{2. If you just want a generic SMEFT for learning}

From your \lstinline|display modellist| output you have several SMEFT/EFT UFOs, for example:
\begin{itemize}
\item \lstinline|SMEFTsim_A_general_MwScheme_UFO_v2|
\item \lstinline|SMEFTsim_general_MwScheme_UFO|
\item {[...]}
\end{itemize}

For a \textbf{generic, reasonably standard SMEFT playground}, I suggest you start with:

\begin{bashblock}
import model SMEFTsim_A_general_MwScheme_UFO_v2
\end{bashblock}

Reason:
\begin{itemize}
\item It’s a widely used SMEFT implementation.
\item It includes a broad set of dimension-6 operators, so you can practice importing the model, inspecting new parameters, and changing Wilson coefficients.
\item It’s not tied to one very narrow corner of parameter space (like 4-top only).
\end{itemize}

[...]

\textbf{3. How this ties into Tutorial 5}

Once you pick one of these models: [...]

If you tell me which physics sector your project cares about (top/EW/VBS/DM/\dots), I can narrow this further and point you to a more targeted model from your list.
\end{promptblock}
%
After loading the model and defining the Drell-Yan process, we ask \ma to help us understand the BSM-operators
%
\begin{userblock}[User]
I have loaded the model SMEFTsim\_A\_general\_MwScheme\_UFO\_v2 and generated a Drell–Yan-like process. However, when I inspect \lstinline|param_card.dat|, I’m overwhelmed by the sheer number of parameters. Please find an interesting BSM parameter (for this tutorial) and explain the physics behind it.
\end{userblock}
%
The \ma provide us with a guide to understand the UFO parameter cards,
%
\begin{promptblock}[\ma (shortened)]
A good ``first'' BSM parameter for a Drell--Yan tutorial in \lstinline|SMEFTsim_A_general_MwScheme_UFO_v2| is one of the \textbf{four-fermion contact operators that couple quarks and leptons}, e.g.\ the coefficient of the operator usually called
\[
\mathcal O_{lq}^{(3)} \sim (\bar l_L \gamma_\mu \tau^I l_L)(\bar q_L \gamma^\mu \tau^I q_L),
\]
where $l_L$ is the lepton doublet and $q_L$ is the quark doublet.

\textbf{1. Why this is a good choice for Drell--Yan}

For a process like
\[
pp \to \ell^+ \ell^-
\]
in the SM you mainly have $\gamma^*/Z$ exchange:
\begin{itemize}
\item $q \bar q \to \gamma^*/Z \to \ell^+ \ell^-.$
\end{itemize}

When you turn on an $\mathcal O_{lq}^{(3)}$--type coefficient:
\begin{itemize}
\item You add a \textbf{contact interaction} of the form
\[
(\bar q_L \gamma_\mu q_L)(\bar \ell_L \gamma^\mu \ell_L)
\]
(up to isospin structure),
\item This contributes a new \textbf{tree-level amplitude} that interferes with the SM $\gamma^*/Z$ exchange.
\end{itemize}

[...]

\textbf{2. How to locate such a parameter in the \lstinline|param_card.dat|}

In \lstinline|SMEFTsim_A_general_MwScheme_UFO_v2| the EFT coefficients are organized by \textbf{operator type and generation indices}. Exact names depend on the implementation, but:
\begin{itemize}
\item Look for a \textbf{BLOCK} that collects SMEFT coefficients (often something like \lstinline|BLOCK SMEFT| or blocks with names containing \lstinline|CLQ3|, \lstinline|CLQ1|, etc.).
\item Inside that block you’ll find entries corresponding to $O_{lq}^{(1)}$ and $O_{lq}^{(3)}$ with generation indices. For Drell--Yan to light charged leptons, you care about:
\begin{itemize}
\item quark generations 1 and 2 (u, d, s, c) in the proton,
\item lepton generations 1 and 2 (e, $\mu$).
\end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

[...]

\textbf{3. What to do with it in the tutorial (qualitatively)}

Once you’ve identified such a coefficient: [...]
\end{promptblock}
%
In case the user cannot locate such an parameter, \ma can be asked to help:
%
\begin{userblock}[User]
I am unable to find those operators in the UFO model.... Please find them for me.
\end{userblock}
%
\ma invoke the Script-Operator to locate the given parameter and responds
%
\begin{promptblock}[\ma (shortened)]
A concrete, interesting choice for Drell--Yan in \lstinline|SMEFTsim_A_general_MwScheme_UFO_v2| is one of the \textbf{four-fermion lepton--quark contact operators}. A representative example (as it appears in the SMEFTsim implementation) is a coefficient called
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\lstinline|clq1Ph1112|} in \textbf{\lstinline|BLOCK FRBLOCK|} of the \lstinline|param_card.dat|.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{1. How to find it in your \lstinline|param_card.dat|}

[...]

\textbf{2. What \lstinline|clq1Ph1112| means physically}

\lstinline|clq1Ph...| coefficients belong to the operator family usually called
\[
O_{lq}^{(1)} \sim (\bar l_L \gamma_\mu l_L)(\bar q_L \gamma^\mu q_L),
\]
where $l_L$ is the left-handed lepton doublet and $q_L$ is the left-handed quark doublet. The suffix \lstinline|1112| encodes a particular light-generation flavor combination (first-generation leptons and light quarks) in the SMEFTsim naming scheme.

For neutral-current Drell--Yan $pp \to \ell^+\ell^-$:
\begin{itemize}
\item In the SM the dominant amplitude is $q\bar q \to \gamma^*/Z \to \ell^+\ell^-.$
\item Turning on \lstinline|clq1Ph1112| adds a \textbf{contact interaction} $(\bar q_L \gamma_\mu q_L)(\bar \ell_L \gamma^\mu \ell_L)$.
\item This new amplitude \textbf{interferes} with the SM $\gamma/Z$ exchange.
\end{itemize}

Qualitatively, this has two main effects:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{High-mass tail enhancement/distortion:} [...]
\item \textbf{Angular-distribution changes:} [...]
\end{enumerate}

\end{promptblock}
%
After launching three \mg runs with different \lstinline|clq1Ph1112| values, we ask \ma to verify the correctness of the users steps
%
\begin{userblock}[User]
I have executed three runs in which I changed the value of `clq1Ph1112`. Please verify that I did this correctly.    
\end{userblock}
%
\ma inspect the user's CLI and the run-specific directories and confirm
%
\begin{promptblock}[\ma]
You did it correctly.

\begin{itemize}
\item Your Drell--Yan process directory is \lstinline|DY_EFT| under \lstinline|/output/tutorials/my_work/05_bsm_eft_models| and it indeed uses the model \lstinline|SMEFTsim_A_general_MwScheme_UFO_v2|.
\item The master \lstinline|Cards/param_card.dat| in \lstinline|DY_EFT| contains \lstinline|clq1Ph1112| (labelled \lstinline|clq1ph1112| in \lstinline|BLOCK FRBLOCK|), so you edited the right parameter.
\item For the three runs \lstinline|run_01|, \lstinline|run_02|, and \lstinline|run_03| under \lstinline|DY_EFT/Events/|:
\begin{itemize}
\item Each run’s configuration (stored in its \lstinline|run_0x_tag_1_banner.txt|) has an entry for \lstinline|clq1ph1112|.
\item The value of \lstinline|clq1Ph1112| is different in each of the three runs.
\end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

So you have three distinct runs of the same Drell--Yan EFT process that differ only in the chosen value of \lstinline|clq1Ph1112|, exactly as intended for the tutorial. If you like, next we can discuss how to set up a simple comparison of the dilepton invariant-mass distributions between these runs (staying at a qualitative level).
\end{promptblock}

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsubsection{Learning by doing}
\label{sec:apps_train_learn}

In case a user knows basic \mg, the \ma can lead them through their task and provide relevant information while working on it. For example, we want to generate events for the process
%
\begin{align}
    p p \to W^+ H 
    \to (\ell^+ \nu_\ell) \, (b \bar{b}) 
    \qquad \text{with} \qquad 
    \ell^+ = e^+, \mu^+ \; .
\end{align}
%
For a first \mg attempt, we execute
%
\begin{bashblock}
define l+ = e+ mu+
define vl = ve vm
generate p p > w+ h, (w+ > l+ vl) (h > b b~)
\end{bashblock}
%
In the last line, a comma is missing between both decay processes, leading to a \mg error. We query \ma

\begin{userblock}[User]
Why do I get this error?
\end{userblock}
%
\ma checks if this vague statement might refer to the CLI. They find and analyze the error and give the solution

\begin{promptblock}[\ma]
You get that error because of a syntax problem in the \texttt{generate} line, not because of the physics of the process.

You wrote:
\begin{bashblock}
generate p p > w+ h, (w+ > l+ vl) (h > b b~)
\end{bashblock}

After the main process \texttt{p p > w+ h}, \mg expects \textbf{each decay block to be separated by commas} and to be a valid process on its own. Here there is a missing comma between the two decay blocks, so MG5 tries to parse them as one malformed block and hits the deprecated \texttt{> A A >} pattern, giving the
\begin{bashblock}
InvalidCmd : Invalid "> A A >" syntax
\end{bashblock}
message.

Use one of these equivalent, valid syntaxes:
\begin{bashblock}
generate p p > w+ h, w+ > l+ vl, h > b b~
\end{bashblock}
or
\begin{bashblock}
generate p p > w+ h, (w+ > l+ vl), (h > b b~)
\end{bashblock}

Both correspond to $pp \to W^+ H$ with $W^+ \to \ell^+ \nu_\ell$ and $H \to b\bar b$.

\end{promptblock}
%


As a second example, we want to generate events for the process 
%
\begin{align}
    p p \to Z H 
    \to (e^+ e^-) \, (Z Z) \; .
\end{align}
%
In \mg, we execute
\begin{bashblock}
generate p p > z h, (h > z z, z > e+ e-)
\end{bashblock}
%
This command defines the different physical process, in which the produced $Z$ does not decay at all, and the produced $H$ decays to $H \to Z Z$, where one $Z$ decays leptonically, $Z \to e^+ e^-$. We ask \ma to check whether the generated process matches the desired process:
%
\begin{userblock}[User]
Please verify whether my defined process corresponds to the process $p p \to Z H$, where the produced $Z$ decays to $Z \to e^+ e^-$ and the produced H to $H \rightarrow Z Z$.
\end{userblock}
%
\ma inspect the user's CLI, decide that the user request refers to it, and reply
%
\begin{promptblock}[\ma]
From your MG5 transcript, the process you defined is

\begin{bashblock}
generate p p > z h, (h > z z, z > e+ e-)
\end{bashblock}

This corresponds to

\begin{itemize}[label=\textbullet]
  \item production: \(pp \to Z_\text{assoc} H\)
  \item Higgs decay: \(H \to Z Z\)
  \item \textbf{one of the Z bosons from the Higgs decay} then decays as \(Z \to e^+ e^-\),
  \item while the \textbf{associated Z from production remains undecayed}.
\end{itemize}

So it does \textbf{not} exactly match the verbal description “\(pp \to ZH\) with the produced \(Z \to e^+ e^-\) and the produced \(H \to ZZ\) (with those Z bosons left stable)”. Instead, it decays a Z coming from the Higgs.

To get what you described (associated Z leptonically decaying, Higgs to ZZ with both Z from H stable), you should use

\begin{bashblock}
generate p p > z h, (z > e+ e-), (h > z z)
\end{bashblock}

Here:
\begin{itemize}[label=\textbullet]
  \item \texttt{(z > e+ e-)} attaches the decay to the \textbf{associated Z} in \texttt{p p > z h}.
  \item \texttt{(h > z z)} gives \(H \to Z Z\) with both Z’s left undecayed.
\end{itemize}

If you later also want to decay the Higgs Z’s, you’d extend the Higgs decay chain, e.g.

\begin{bashblock}
generate p p > z h, (z > e+ e-), (h > z z, z > e+ e-, z > mu+ mu-)
\end{bashblock}

which corresponds to \(Z_\text{assoc} \to e^+ e^-\) and the two Z’s from H going to \(e^+ e^-\) and \(\mu^+ \mu^-\), respectively.
\end{promptblock}
% 
We emphasize that in the last step \ma go beyond the requested process and proposes a reasonable refinement of the simulation.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsubsection{Documented reweighted simulation}
\label{sec:apps_train_doc}

Yet another way \ma support users is a human-readable documentation of complex tasks. We use an example from the SFitter workflow~\cite{Elmer:2023wtr}:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item generate two parton-level $t \bar{t}$ datasets, one at LO and one at NLO;
    \item For each dataset, bin the events in $m_{t \bar{t}}$ and compute a bin-wise $K$-factor by reweighting events from LO to NLO;
    \item generate LO events including a chromomagnetic dimension-6 operator, including showering and detector effects;
    \item reweight those events with the $K$-factor determined for the SM-process.
\end{enumerate}
%
A single bash script controls the generation chain. The corresponding scripts are available in the \href{https://github.com/heidelberg-hepml/MadAgents/tree/main/supplementary/dataset_documentation}{code repository}. We invoke this script and save the bash output to a log file. Apart from this, the dataset directory does not contain any documentation and the scripts do not contain comments. We query \ma to create such a  documentation

\begin{userblock}[User]
I have generated the dataset \lstinline|/output/dataset/output/dataset/ufo_ttbar_reweighted.npz| by running \lstinline|/output/dataset/run.sh|.

\textbf{Task:} Create a technically precise documentation in \lstinline|/output/dataset/documentation| by inspecting the directory \lstinline|/output/dataset|.

\textbf{Deliverables:}
\begin{enumerate}
\item \lstinline|/output/dataset/documentation/documentation.md| (detailed)
\item \lstinline|/output/dataset/documentation/summary.md| (short summary of the detailed documentation)
\end{enumerate}

The detailed documentation \textbf{MUST} include:

\textbf{A. Reproducibility / software environment}
\begin{itemize}
\item Core runtime(s) used (e.g., Python/R/Julia/MATLAB) + versions
\item Libraries/frameworks/external tools/dependencies used by dataset generation + versions
\end{itemize}

\textbf{B. Physics behind the dataset}
\begin{itemize}
\item What collider process(es) are simulated, including governing equations (or main model) and assumptions
\item Units, coordinate system, boundary/initial conditions (if applicable)
\item What each data field represents physically
\end{itemize}

\textbf{C. Dataset generation process}
\begin{itemize}
\item High-level pipeline overview (steps), inputs $\to$ outputs mapping, file formats, directory structure
\item Parameterization: what parameters vary and their ranges
\item Randomness: seeds, noise models, determinism/reproducibility
\item Any filtering/post-processing steps
\end{itemize}

\textbf{D. Plots (optional):}
\begin{itemize}
\item Only create plots if they materially improve understanding (e.g., distributions, example trajectories/fields).
\item Save scripts in \lstinline|/output/dataset/documentation/scripts| and plots in \lstinline|/output/dataset/documentation/plots|.
\item Embed the plots in \lstinline|documentation.md| under the relevant section(s), as inline Markdown image links using relative paths.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Restrictions:}
\begin{itemize}
\item Inside \lstinline|/output/dataset|, you are only allowed to create and modify the folder \lstinline|/output/dataset/documentation|.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Style:}
\begin{itemize}
\item Prefer using equations over descriptions.
\item \lstinline|documentation.md| should use clear sections and be thorough but not verbose.
\item \lstinline|summary.md| should be a short structured summary with 2--4 mini-sections (200--500 words).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Goal:}
\begin{itemize}
\item After reading \lstinline|summary.md|, a particle physics PhD student should be able to:
\begin{itemize}
\item identify what physical process(es) the dataset represents,
\item understand the main observables/labels and their units/meaning,
\item understand the intended use-cases and key limitations at a high level.
\item generate a new dataset with the existing scripts.
\end{itemize}
\item After reading \lstinline|documentation.md|, a particle physics PhD student should be able to:
\begin{itemize}
\item reproduce the dataset generation end-to-end on a clean machine (including exact software versions and a verification/smoke test) using only \lstinline|documentation.md| (without access to the original machine environment),
\item interpret every stored field physically (definitions, units, coordinate conventions, and any transformations),
\item understand the modeling assumptions and validity regime (what is included/excluded, approximations, and known biases),
\item determine which physics questions the dataset is suitable/unsuitable for, with explicit examples and failure modes.
\end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\textbf{Prompt explanation:} We carefully specify the requested task, deliverables, including must-have requirements, restrictions, and the documentation style. In addition, we include the intended use of the documentation, which allows \ma to further tailor the documentation.
\end{userblock}
%
\ma develop and execute the multi-step plan
%
\begin{planblock}[Plan]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*, label=\textbf{Step \arabic*:}, itemsep=0.6em]
\item Inspect \lstinline|/output/dataset| structure and key files
\item Reconstruct the software environment and dependencies
\item Analyze physics setup and generation pipeline
\item Inspect \lstinline|.npz| dataset structure and map fields to physical quantities
\item Create optional plots and define verification/smoke tests
\item Write concise summary in \lstinline|summary.md|
\end{enumerate}
\end{planblock}
%
and produce a documentation file
%
\markdownfile[summary.md]{markdown_files/summary.md}
%
The detailed documentation is shown in App.~\ref{app:ds_documentation}, including a few highlights: In addition to listing the software versions, they list the template as well. We uninstalled uproot such that \ma cannot extract the version. In the documentation, this is explicitly stated and a version recommendation is given. \ma find that the top mass and width are (slightly) different between the Standard Model and UFO runs. The $K$-factor construction is described in detail. A table summarizes the layout of the final dataset, including data types and units.

\clearpage
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection{Supporting experienced users}
\label{sec:apps_adv} 

\ma do not only support users with or without basic knowledge of \mg, but also experienced users who want to perform more advanced tasks. As an example, we consider a scenario where we want to generate high-precision production events  
%
\begin{align}
 pp \to t \bar{t} 
 \qquad \text{(production beyond on-shell/LO, no decays)} \; .
\end{align}
%
We assume that the LO simulations are not sufficient and prompt \ma on more precise simulations:

\begin{userblock}[User]
I am a PhD student working on precision simulations for top-quark processes at colliders.\\
I currently have a baseline leading-order, on-shell simulation for a given process and observable.\\

\textbf{Task:}\\ 
Critically assess the limitations of such a setup and propose systematic and conceptually distinct ways to improve its theoretical precision.

For each proposed improvement:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Explain which physical effects are being captured that are missing at baseline level.
    \item Clarify when and why this improvement becomes important for realistic analyses.
    \item Indicate, at a high level, how such an improvement is typically implemented in modern Monte-Carlo simulations.
\end{itemize}

Reason from first principles and standard practice in collider phenomenology. The goal is to identify the dominant axes along which precision is improved, not to enumerate tools.
\end{userblock}
%
\begin{promptblock}[MadAgents (shortened)]
[\ldots]\\ 
\textbf{Dominant and conceptually distinct precision axes}\\
Starting from a leading-order, on-shell baseline for top-quark processes, the main orthogonal directions along which theoretical predictions can be systematically improved are:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Perturbative QCD accuracy in production and decay:}
  fixed-order calculations at NLO or NNLO, matching to parton showers (NLO+PS), and higher-order resummation at NNLO accuracy.

  \item \textbf{Description of additional hard radiation:}
  inclusion of multi-jet matrix elements and their consistent merging with parton showers at LO or NLO accuracy.

  \item \textbf{Finite-width and off-shell effects:}
  treatment beyond the narrow-width approximation, including the full resonance structure, non-resonant contributions, and interference effects.

  \item \textbf{Electroweak and QED corrections:}
  NLO electroweak effects and photon radiation, which become increasingly relevant for high-scale observables and leptonic final states.

  \item \textbf{All-order logarithmic structure:}
  higher-logarithmic resummation and improved parton-shower descriptions for observables sensitive to large scale hierarchies.

  \item \textbf{Non-perturbative, PDF, and scheme uncertainties:}
  effects from hadronisation, the underlying event, colour reconnection, parton distribution functions and $\alpha_s$, as well as choices of mass and renormalisation schemes.
\end{enumerate}

In realistic precision top-quark analyses, several of these axes are typically combined. The appropriate level of sophistication is guided by which physical effects dominate the theoretical uncertainties for the observables and phase-space regions under consideration.

\end{promptblock} 
%
\ma provide a list of what they consider the essential options to improve the precision, along with short explanations. We show only the final summary for the six proposed improvements, including NLO, additional jets from the matrix elements, as well as off-shell effects. Although a more experienced user has some level of familiarity with these options, they are unsure which would be suitable for their purpose. We prompt \ma to help with this decision, ask for a small number of events to be generated for each option, and a comparison of the results.

\begin{userblock}[User (shortened)]
Based on the precision-improvement axes identified above, the task is to construct and compare concrete simulation setups.

\textbf{Simulation task:}
For each of the following strategies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Higher-order QCD corrections (NLO+PS),
  \item Finite-width and off-shell effects beyond the narrow-width approximation,
  \item Hard additional radiation via matrix-element multi-jet merging,
\end{enumerate}
do the following:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Define a representative simulation setup implementing the respective strategy.
  \item Generate 50000 events for each setup using reasonable, standard choices.
  \item Clearly state all assumptions and simplifications, such as on-shell versus off-shell decays, jet multiplicities, or matching and merging schemes.
\end{itemize}

You may choose suitable tools, schemes, and defaults where needed, but avoid unnecessary over-optimisation.
[\ldots]
\end{userblock}
%
\begin{promptblock}[MadAgents (shortened)]
\subsubsection*{Concrete setups and files}

\textbf{Common to all three:}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Collider: pp at $\sqrt{s}=13$ TeV.
  \item $m_t = 172.5$ GeV, $\Gamma_t \approx 1.32$ GeV.
  \item PDFs: NNPDF3.1 NLO (LHAPDF ID 303400).
  \item Final state used in the comparison: \textbf{only} $t\bar t\to b e^+\nu_e\, \bar b \mu^-\bar\nu_\mu$ (no $e^-\mu^+$).
  \item Fiducial selection (analysis level): exactly 1 e and 1 $\mu$ (OS), $p_T>25$ GeV, $|\eta|<2.5$, isolated; at least 2 jets with $p_T>25$ GeV, $|\eta|<2.5$ and at least 1 truth b-tag.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Samples and locations:}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{NLO+PS on-shell (higher-order QCD axis)}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Process: $pp \to t\bar t$ [QCD] at \textbf{NLO QCD}, on-shell tops.
    \item Decays: LO MadSpin, $t\to b e^+\nu_e$, $\bar t\to \bar b\mu^-\bar\nu_\mu$.
    \item Matching: aMC@NLO to Pythia8, hadronisation+UE on. [\ldots]
  \end{itemize}

  \item \textbf{LO+PS off-shell 2$\to$6 (off-shell axis)}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Process: $pp \to e^+\nu_e\,\mu^-\bar\nu_\mu\,b\bar b$ at \textbf{LO QCD}, with complex-mass scheme and all doubly-, singly- and non-resonant diagrams (full $W^+W^-b\bar b$ structure).
    \item No separate decay step: leptons and b’s appear directly from the ME, with finite widths.
    \item Shower: Pythia8, hadronisation+UE on. [\ldots]
  \end{itemize}

  \item \textbf{LO MLM-merged $t\bar t$(+0,1j)+PS (hard-radiation axis)}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Processes: $pp \to t\bar t$, $pp \to t\bar t j$ at \textbf{LO QCD} (0 and 1 extra parton; 2-jet MEs were dropped due to a technical issue).
    \item Merging: MLM $k_T$ with Pythia8, $x_{\text{cut}} \approx 30$ GeV, dynamic scale $\sim H_T/4$.
    \item Decays: LO MadSpin to the same $e^+\mu^-$ channel as in NLO+PS. [\ldots]
  \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Unified analysis and plots:}
\begin{itemize}
  \item {[\ldots]}
  \item Plots (PDF+PNG): \texttt{plots/compare\_ttbar\_*.pdf} (Njets, pT\_lep1, pT\_lep2, pT\_ttbar, M\_ttbar, M\_bl, with log-y variants for Njets and pT\_ttbar).
\end{itemize}

[\ldots]\\
If you want, we can next pick one observable (e.g.\ $p_{T,t\bar t}$) and go through the plots and numbers in more detail, or adapt the selections to a concrete analysis you’re working on.
\end{promptblock}
%
The response by the \ma summarizes their workflow effectively. They begin by specifying parameters common among all simulations such as collider energy, final state particles and analysis level cuts.
This is both efficient and ensures comparability between simulations. 
They continue with the settings for the individual simulations. Here, \ma realizes that the produced tops need to be decayed if the on-shell simulations are to be compared with the off-shell production. For this \ma decides to implement the top decays via MadSpin~\cite{Artoisenet:2012st}.\\
Not shown in its reply but nonetheless worth mentioning is that, because we originally only provide a minimal \mg environment, the NLO simulation requires additional packages be installed. Again, \ma show that they are able to install all necessary dependencies autonomously, as part of its workflow.
Finally, without any additional user input, \ma select relevant kinematic observables, including $p_{T}^{\ell_1},p_{T}^{\ell_2},p_{T}^{t\bar{t}}$, the invariant mass $m_{t\bar{t}}$, and the number of jets $N_\text{jets}$. After running all simulations, \ma analyze the events and create histograms for these observables.
In Fig.~\ref{fig:ttbar_comp} we show the invariant mass $m_{t\bar{t}}$ and the transverse momentum of the top pair $p_{T}^{t\bar{t}}$ created autonomously.  We observe differences especially for the transverse momentum, but we want to know more and prompt \ma to analyze and discuss the differences.

%-------------------------------------------
\begin{figure}[t]
    \includegraphics[width=0.495\linewidth]{figs/examples/compare_ttbar_50k_pT_ttbar_hist_shape.pdf}
    \includegraphics[width=0.495\linewidth]{figs/examples/compare_ttbar_50k_M_ttbar_hist_shape.pdf}
    \caption{Comparison of the $m_{t\bar{t}}$ and $p_{T}^{t\bar{t}}$ distributions for precision simulations of the production process, figure generated autonomously by \ma.} 
    \label{fig:ttbar_comp}
\end{figure}
%-------------------------------------------

\begin{userblock}[User]
Focusing on the plots you have generated, examine in detail the normalized transverse-momentum distribution of the $p_{T,t\bar{t}}$.

\textbf{Task:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Compare the shapes of the distributions obtained with the different simulation strategies.
    \item Identify and explain the dominant physical effects responsible for the observed differences in shape.
    \item Discuss how these differences impact the interpretation of measurements or fits.
\end{itemize}
\end{userblock}
%

\begin{promptblock}[MadAgents (shortened)]
\subsubsection*{Qualitative comparison of shapes}

\textbf{NLO+PS on-shell vs LO+PS off-shell $2\to6$.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Once you normalise each curve to unit area, the shapes are \textbf{very similar} over most of the $p_{T,t\bar t}$ range. [\ldots]
\end{itemize}

\textbf{LO merged $\bar t t(+0,1j)+\mathrm{PS}$ vs the above two.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item The \textbf{merged} sample is visibly \textbf{harder} in $p_{T,t\bar t}$
  [\ldots]
  \item After unit-area normalisation, the merged curve sits \textbf{below} NLO at small $p_T$ and \textbf{above} it in the tail.
\end{itemize}
[\ldots]
\vspace{1em}
\hrule

\subsubsection*{Dominant physical effects behind these differences}
\textbf{(a) Why NLO+PS on-shell and LO off-shell shapes are so similar}

\begin{itemize}
  \item In both cases, the \textbf{transverse recoil} of the $t\bar t$ system is governed primarily by \textbf{QCD radiation}: the first hard emission and the subsequent shower.
  \item The off-shell $2\to6$ sample differs from on-shell NLO mainly by its \textbf{resonance structure}:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item It includes doubly-resonant, singly-resonant, and non-resonant diagrams with finite top and $W$ widths.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
[\ldots]\\
\textbf{(b) Why the LO merged $\bar t t(+0,1j)+\mathrm{PS}$ spectrum is harder}\\
Here the key difference is not off-shellness or perturbative order per se, but the \textbf{matrix-element description of hard extra radiation}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item In the merged sample you have explicit LO matrix elements for
  \[
    pp \to t\bar t \quad \text{and} \quad pp \to t\bar t + j,
  \]
  combined with MLM matching. This means:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item The \textbf{first hard jet} is described by the \textbf{exact tree-level ME} rather than purely by the shower.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
[\ldots]

\vspace{1em}
\hrule

\subsubsection*{Impact on measurements and fits}
\textbf{(a) Measurements of $\mathrm{d}\sigma/\mathrm{d}p_{T,t\bar t}$}

For a differential $t\bar t$ measurement in $p_T$:
\begin{itemize}
  \item If you use \textbf{NLO+PS on-shell} as your baseline, you get:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Correct inclusive normalisation (at NLO) and a decent shape at low--moderate $p_T$.
    \item Some residual modelling uncertainty in the \textbf{tail}, where multiple hard emissions are important.
  \end{itemize}

  \item If you use only \textbf{LO+PS off-shell} without merging:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item You gain a better description of off-shell line shapes, but
    \item You still suffer from LO-only radiation in production; in the $p_T$ tail, uncertainties are large and the shape can deviate from higher-order predictions.
  \end{itemize}

  \item If you use \textbf{LO merged $\bar t t(+0,1j)+\mathrm{PS}$}:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item You get a much more physical shape in the region where a single hard extra jet dominates the recoil.
    \item However, the normalisation is still LO; for serious comparisons you’d prefer NLO merged (or at least NLO+LO-merged) setups.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

[\ldots]\\
\textbf{(b) Fits to PDFs and EFT / BSM interpretations}\\
Both PDF fits and EFT/BSM searches often look for deviations in the \textbf{high-$p_T$ tail}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item If the SM prediction \textbf{underestimates} the tail (e.g.\ pure LO+PS without merging, or inadequate treatment of higher multiplicities), you might misinterpret an excess as evidence for new physics or anomalous couplings when it’s just missing hard QCD radiation.
  \item If the SM prediction \textbf{overestimates} the tail (e.g.\ some LO merged setups can overshoot in particular regions if scales/merging parameters are not well chosen), you could conversely weaken limits or miss real deviations.
\end{itemize}

[\ldots]\\
In short: for the \textbf{normalized} $p_{T,t\bar t}$ distribution, the main driver of shape differences is whether you include \textbf{matrix-element multi-jet information} (the merged sample), not whether you use off-shell vs on-shell top kinematics at fixed order. That has direct consequences for how you model high-$p_T$ tails in measurements, PDF fits, and EFT/BSM interpretations; off-shell effects then become important in more specialized, kinematic-edge or interference-dominated observables rather than in a standard, central $p_{T,t\bar t}$ spectrum.
\end{promptblock}
%
\ma reply with a detailed discussion of the plot. They begin by visually analyzing the differences between the methods, pointing out both similarities and differences between the approaches, while also providing a brief discussion of possible causes from physics effects.
Particularly interesting are also the physics scenarios \ma independently came up with. They begin by discussing Standard Model precision measurements, list the pros and cons of each approach and give suggestions which is best depending on their purpose. 
It recognizes that the simulations mostly differ in the tails and extends the discussion to PDF and EFT fits, where the proper modeling of these tails is essential. This illustrates how \ma can help users interpret results in the context of physics scenarios they themselves might be unfamiliar with, such as PDF and EFT fits in this case.
\clearpage
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection{Autonomous event generation}
\label{sec:apps_auto}

Motivated by the workflow suggested by HEPTAPOD~\cite{Menzo:2025cim}, we perform a scan over masses for a simple scalar leptoquark model. As input we provide \ma with a pdf file containing only the information provided in the example workflow section of their paper. This includes a brief description of the leptoquark model and its decays, as well as a rough outline of the workflow. From parton-level generation with \mg to showering and hadronization with \pythia, jet clustering with \fastjet, and the reconstruction of the minimum leptoquark mass defined as
%
\begin{align}
    m_{\mathrm{LQ}}^{\mathrm{min}} = \min \left\{m_{\mathrm{LQ}}^{(1)},m_{\mathrm{LQ}}^{(2)}\right\} \; .
\end{align}
%
We do not provide any specific information on how to perform this task, we only outline the steps and tools to be used and the following prompt. The exact file used can be found \href{https://github.com/heidelberg-hepml/MadAgents/blob/main/supplementary/autonomous_generation/heptapod_input.pdf}{here}.

\begin{userblock}[User]
Please implement and execute the workflow described in the paper.
As a final deliverable, I want the generated events, a histogram of the resonance reconstruction, and a bash script that can execute the end-to-end workflow (including creation of the resonance histogram).
All deliverables must be saved, you may create suitable subdirectories.\\

\textbf{Constraint}:
Your generated events and reconstructions must match the paper as closely as possible.\\

\textbf{Persistence}:
Do not present partial or simplified results. Present the final results only if you believe you have recreated the workflow to the best of your ability and that the outputs match the paper as closely as reasonably possible.\\

\textbf{Assumptions \& Decisions}:
If any part of the paper is unclear or ambiguous, do not ask me. Instead, make a reasonable decision. In your final reply, list every unclear or ambiguous point you encountered and, for each one, state the decision you made and briefly explain your reasoning.\\

\textbf{Permission}:
You are allowed to install any missing dependencies. Do not ask me for installation decisions; make reasonable decisions yourself.

\end{userblock}
%
Since this is a non-trivial task \ma decides that a step-by-step plan needs to be created.

\begin{planblock}[Plan]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*, label=\textbf{Step \arabic*:}, itemsep=0.6em]

\item Locate PDF and set up project directories.

\item Extract physics and workflow specifications from the PDF and references.

\item Inspect and prepare the software environment.

\item Implement MadGraph and event generation workflow.

\item Implement reconstruction, resonance analysis, and histogram production.

\item Create end-to-end bash workflow script.

\item Validate full workflow and prepare final deliverables and ambiguity summary.

\end{enumerate}
    
\end{planblock}
%
For this complex task, the Orchestrator uses the Script-Operator to set up a well-defined environment to work in, to make the workflow as smooth as possible. Afterwards, the PDF-Reader searches the pdf file for the information needed, after which the Script-Operator logs ambiguities. In the next step, the MG-Operator checks if all required tools are installed, installing missing dependencies as needed. In this example, the only missing tool is the UFO file for the leptoquark model. To remedy this, \ma launch a web search to find a public UFO model.

With the setup established, \ma begin with the implementation of the \mg workflow. This includes writing the \texttt{run\_cards}, \texttt{param\_cards}, and other configuration files. They are tested and debugged through low-statistics test runs, to confirm that they generate valid events. They are further inspected by the Reviewer to ensure that they are consistent with the specifications provided in the input. Once events are generated, they need to be analyzed. The Script-Operator writes the reconstruction code, followed by the Plotter for visualization. Again, the Reviewer checks its validity. Finally, \ma create an end-to-end bash script for easy reproduction and validation of the workflow. Once this is done, all requested outputs, \eg event files, figures, and code used for the analysis, are consolidated into a single output folder.

%-----------------------------------------
\begin{figure}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{figs/examples/heptapod_mLQmin_comparison.pdf}
    \caption{Reconstructed minimum leptoquark mass for $m_{S1} \in [1, 1.5, 2]$ TeV, figure generated autonomously by \ma.}
    \label{fig:HEPTAPOD_comp}
\end{figure}
%-----------------------------------------

Figure~\ref{fig:HEPTAPOD_comp} shows the minimum reconstructed invariant mass of the leptoquark, created completely autonomously by the Plotter. We can clearly see the  resonance peaks for each simulated leptoquark mass. Although this simulation chain is executed autonomously, \ma are not meant to replace the human user. It should support and teach the user how to complete these tasks. To this end, we prompt \ma to give details on the essential steps. \ma not only return a concise summary, but also point the user to the scripts and notes it created for more details. That way, the user is provided with all information needed to understand the simulations, validate them and implement adjustments as needed.

\begin{userblock}[User]
    Please generate a clear, well-structured summary of the most important steps you carried out and the key decisions you made, including appropriate citations, written in a style suitable for direct inclusion in a LaTeX document.
\end{userblock}

\begin{promptblock}[MadAgents (shortened)]
\textbf{Simulation and analysis setup}\\
The signal under study is a simplified scalar leptoquark $S_1$ with quantum numbers
$S_1 \sim (\bar{3},1,1/3)$, pair-produced in proton--proton collisions,
followed by the decay chain
%
\begin{align}
  pp \to S_1 S_1^{\dagger} \to (\ell^+ j)(\ell^- j)
  \;
  \Rightarrow \;
  2\ell + 2j~\text{final state},
\end{align}
closely following the workflow outlined in the HEPTAPOD~\cite{Menzo:2025cim} short note and the
MC4BSM-style tutorials.\\

\textbf{Event generation.}\\
Events are generated with \texttt{MadGraph5\_aMC@NLO} v3.7.0~\cite{Alwall:2014hca}
interfaced to \texttt{Pythia~8.3}~\cite{Sjostrand:2014zea} for parton showering and
hadronisation. We use an existing public leptoquark UFO model,
\texttt{LQnlo\_5FNS\_v5\_UFO}, originally constructed in the
\texttt{FeynRules} framework~\cite{Alloul:2013bka}, and identify the HEPTAPOD
scalar $S_1$ with the particle \texttt{LQ1d} (and its antiparticle
\texttt{LQ1d\textasciitilde{}}). The collider setup is
\begin{itemize}
  \item proton--proton collisions at $\sqrt{s} = 8~\text{TeV}$, implemented as
        $E_{\text{beam}1} = E_{\text{beam}2} = 4000~\text{GeV}$;
  \item leading-order (LO) matrix elements with parton shower (LO+PS),
        no ME--PS matching;
  \item PDFs: LHAPDF6~\cite{Buckley:2014ana} with the NNPDF3.1 LO set
        NNPDF31\_lo\_as\_0130~\cite{Ball:2017nwa} (\texttt{lhaid} = 315200).
\end{itemize}

The leptoquark mass is scanned over three benchmark points
%
\begin{align}
  m_{S_1} \in \{1.0, 1.5, 2.0\}~\text{TeV}.
\end{align}
%
For each mass point, we generate $N_{\text{events}} = 20\,000$ unweighted LO
parton-level events and shower them with Pythia~8.  The total width of $S_1$ is
taken from the UFO model defaults (narrow-width regime, $\Gamma/m \lesssim 1\%$).
A simple flavour structure is chosen by turning on only the right-handed
Yukawa coupling between first-generation up quarks and electrons,
%
\begin{align}
  y_{11} \ne 0,\quad y_{ij} = 0~\text{for}~(i,j) \neq (1,1),
\end{align}
%
so that $S_1$ decays dominantly as $S_1 \to e^+ u$ and $S_1^{\dagger} \to e^- \bar u$.
SM backgrounds (DY+jets, $t\bar t$, dibosons) are not simulated in this
implementation; the focus is on the signal resonance shape and its dependence on
$m_{S_1}$.

\textbf{Hadron-level reconstruction.}\\
The analysis is performed at hadron level, using Pythia~8 final states without
additional detector simulation.  Stable final-state particles are taken as those
with \texttt{status} $=1$ in the HepMC record~\cite{Dobbs:2001ck}. Neutrinos
($\lvert \text{PID} \rvert \in \{12,14,16\}$) are removed from both lepton and
jet reconstruction.

Electrons are reconstructed from HepMC particles with $\lvert \text{PID} \rvert = 11$,
with kinematic cuts
\begin{align}
  p_T^e > 50~\text{GeV},\qquad \lvert \eta^e \rvert < 2.5,
\end{align}
and we require exactly two such electrons per event.

Jets are clustered from all remaining stable particles (excluding neutrinos and
electrons) using the anti-$k_T$ algorithm~\cite{Cacciari:2008gp} with radius
parameter $R=0.4$ as implemented in the \texttt{FastJet} framework
(via the Python wrapper \texttt{pyjet})~\cite{Cacciari:2011ma}. Reconstructed jets
must satisfy
\begin{align}
  p_T^j > 50~\text{GeV},\qquad \lvert \eta^j \rvert < 5.0,
\end{align}
and at least two jets are required. Only the two leading jets in $p_T$ are used
for the resonance reconstruction.

\textbf{Resonance reconstruction and $m_{LQ}^{\min}$.}\\
In events with exactly two selected electrons $(\ell_1, \ell_2)$ and at least
two selected jets $(j_1, j_2)$, we reconstruct two leptoquark candidates by
forming all possible pairings between leptons and the two leading jets:
%
\begin{align}
  \text{Pairing A}: &\quad (\ell_1 + j_1),~(\ell_2 + j_2),\\
  \text{Pairing B}: &\quad (\ell_1 + j_2),~(\ell_2 + j_1).
\end{align}
%
For each pairing we compute the invariant masses $(m_1, m_2)$ of the two
candidates. We select the pairing that minimises $\lvert m_1 - m_2 \rvert$, and
from that pairing define the reconstructed leptoquark mass variable
%
\begin{align}
  m_\text{LQ}^{\min} = \min(m_1, m_2).
\end{align}
%
This follows the standard HEPTAPOD/MC4BSM prescription and produces a
resonance-like peak near the true $m_{S_1}$ with a modest low-mass tail. For a
broader discussion of scalar leptoquark phenomenology and typical benchmark
choices, see e.g. Ref.~\cite{Dorsner:2016wpm}.

\textbf{Histogramming and normalisation.}\\
The following binning of $m_{LQ}^{\min}$ is used:
50 bins from $0$ to $2500~\text{GeV}$ with unit-area normalisation per mass point, i.e. each distribution is normalised such that $\sum_i N_i = 1$. The bin contents can be interpreted as probabilities per bin (or, equivalently, as a discretised probability density when divided by the bin width). We overlay the three benchmark spectra ($m_{S_1}=1.0,1.5,2.0$~TeV) for direct comparison. Poisson uncertainties $\sqrt{N_i}$ are propagated to the normalised histograms and shown as error bars.

[\ldots]\\
All non-trivial choices that are not fixed by the HEPTAPOD note (collider
energy, PDF set, specific UFO model, Yukawa pattern, use of hadron-level
rather than detector-level objects, exact cuts and binning) are documented in a
separate ``ambiguities and decisions'' note that accompanies this analysis.
\end{promptblock}


\clearpage
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Outlook}
\label{sec:outlook}

Modern ML methods allow us to combine the two challenges of LHC physics, the vast amount of data and its complexity, into a unique opportunity. First-principle theory simulations in the form of event generators allow us to formulate our understanding of the LHC in terms of a fundamental physics Lagrangian. If we want to use ML-enhanced simulation based inference to extract all available physics from the LHC dataset, the numerical tools and the analysis workflow both have to become more precise, faster, and more efficient. Agentic use of ultra-fast simulation tools is re-shaping numerical science, so we have explored accelerated event generation, specifically a highly efficient user interface for \mg using \ma:
%
\begin{enumerate}
\item First, we have shown how \ma can autonomously install the \mg software, even including building a \root from the source code.
\item Second, we have illustrated how \ma can communicate with inexperienced users to provide tutorials and support for standard tasks. The user can actively interact with \ma and ask for explanations, for instance, how to re\-weight SMEFT events with $K$-factors computed from the SM.
\item Next, we have demonstrated how \ma provide support to experienced users. They generated events employing non-trivial theory approaches to top pair production ---  NLO production, off-shell tops, and additional matrix element jets --- and analyze the differences in detail.
\item Finally, we have turned to an autonomous task, namely, independently reproducing simulations from a publication. We have shown how \ma reproduce the key figures of the HEPTAPOD publication, including a detailed account of all relevant pieces of information missing from the publication.
\end{enumerate}
%
This study has shown how agent-supported tools like \mg can significantly accelerate LHC research. Autonomous simulations provide an important step towards AI-accelerating not only LHC searches, but also precision analyses and global analyses. At the same time, we have shown how \ma can take over parts of our workflows and at the same time train physicists.

\appendix
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Individual agents} 
\label{app:agentic_details}

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\appsubsection{Controlling agents}

The Orchestrator, Planner and Reviewer share the same conversation history. Upon invocation, each agent first generates reasoning tokens and then outputs its response. Since the reasoning tokens are agent and system prompt specific, we do not insert them into the shared conversation. While there is the option to include the reasoning tokens belonging to the invoked agent on the fly, we have decided against it as we observed that they confuse the Orchestrator: Instead of proceeding with the workflow, the Orchestrator repeatedly called the same agent with almost identical instructions.

As the Reviewer is able to call tools (web-search does not count as tool calls in this sense as we use the \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-web-search}{OpenAI's built-in tool}), we create a temporary copy of the shared conversation and append all tool calls, tool outputs and reasoning tokens. The latter is explicitly \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reasoning#keeping-reasoning-items-in-context}{recommended by OpenAI for tool-calling agents}. After the Reviewer responds, we append only the answer to the shared conversation.

In case the shared conversation becomes too long, those three agents also share a common conversation summary. Again, there is a special case for the Reviewer. If we need to summarize the conversation during its tool calling, the Reviewer obtains a dedicated, temporary conversation summary. Once the Reviewer invocation finishes, a shared summary is created.

As the Plan-Updater simply translates the Orchestrator's requests into structured data, it only observes the Orchestrator's immediate instructions.

We restrict the Orchestrator, Planner and Plan-Updater to generate \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/structured-outputs?utm_source=chatgpt.com}{structured output} and refer to Ref.~\cite{geng2023grammar} for a conceptual introduction. In the following, we present their output schemas and describe how they are appended to the shared conversation.

\paragraph{Orchestrator Output}

The Orchestrator outputs its decisions in the following structured form:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{recipient:} the recipient that the Orchestrator messages/invokes next.
    \item \textbf{reasoning:} a brief explanation for the Orchestrator's decision.
    \item \textbf{message:} the message sent to the recipient. For workers: typically the task instruction.
    \item \textbf{reasoning effort:} the reasoning effort of the invoked agent.\\
    Possible values: "low", "medium", "high".\\
    This allows the Orchestrator to manage the reasoning time/cost depending on the task. The Orchestrator is always invoked with "high" reasoning effort.
    \item \textbf{future note:} a small scratchpad for near-future steps.\\
    This enables the orchestrator to memorize its upcoming steps. We briefly explain why this is useful:\\
    It is not uncommon for the orchestrator to plan a few steps ahead. Once the Orchestrator invokes an agent or responds to the user, its reasoning tokens are stripped, which might contain its short-term plan. Without this scratchpad, the Orchestrator is forced to rethink the upcoming steps every time. Not only does this increase the cost and time, it might even degrade the Orchestrator's performance.\\
    This explanation is similar to OpenAI's explanation for \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reasoning#keeping-reasoning-items-in-context}{keeping the reasoning tokens during tool calling}.
\end{itemize}
The raw Orchestrator output (without reasoning tokens) is inserted into the shared conversation.

\paragraph{Planner and Plan-Updater Output}

The Planner generates a plan as a list of plan steps. Each plan step contains the following fields:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{ID:} a unique identifier.
    \item \textbf{title:} a concise title.
    \item \textbf{description:} of what should be done in this step.
    \item \textbf{rationale:} a brief explanation of why this step exists.
    \item \textbf{depends on:} a list of plan step IDs on which this step depends.
    \item \textbf{status:} of this step.\\
    Possible values: "pending" (can be worked on), "in progress" (currently worked on), "done" (completed), "failed", "skipped", "blocked" (a dependency must be completed or skipped first).
    \item \textbf{outcome:} of the plan step.\\
    If completed, it contains a summary of the results.\\
    If failed, it contains the error(s).\\
    If skipped, it contains an explanation for skipping this step.
\end{itemize}

To update the existing plan, the Plan-Updater outputs a list of updates consisting of the following fields:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{ID: } of the updated step.
    \item \textbf{status: } the updated status.
    \item \textbf{outcome: } the updated outcome. It can be empty.
\end{itemize}
The status and outcome of the corresponding plan steps are overwritten with the updated values.

After creating the plan and after each plan update, we programmatically update the statuses "pending" and "blocked". First, we set every "pending" step to blocked. Then, we inspect each "blocked" step. If all of its dependencies are completed or skipped, we set this plan step to "pending".

The generated plan and the updated plan are appended to the shared conversation with the following messages
%
\begin{planblock}[Planner message]
I have created the following plan:\\
\{\lstinline|json.dumps(plan, indent=2)|\}
\end{planblock}
%
\begin{planblock}[Plan-Updater message]
The updated plan is:\\
\{\lstinline|json.dumps(plan, indent=2)|\}
\end{planblock}

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\appsubsection{Worker agents}

As each Worker only observes its own dedicated conversation, we keep all reasoning tokens, tool calls and tool outputs. This gives the Worker the ability to recall previous chain-of-thoughts and tool interactions (unless they were summarized). This can be useful, for instance, if the Orchestrator instructs the Worker to change a deliverable slightly, if the Worker asks for help to solve an error, or if the user asks for insights into the Worker's reasoning.

To leverage that LLMs are well finetuned for user conversations, we disguise the Orchestrator messages as user messages, giving each Worker the impression that they communicate with a user.


%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\appsubsection{Summarizer}

Before invoking any agent (except for the Plan-Updater) and after any tool call (except for \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-web-search}{OpenAI's built-in web-search tool}), we approximate the token length of the agent's conversation. For each message, we determine the token count from OpenAI's returned usage metadata whenever possible. Otherwise, we resort to a character-length-based approximation for text and a byte-length-based approximation for images and PDF files.

If the conversation exceeds $150$k tokens, we summarize the oldest messages. We keep the last 10 non-tool-output messages and, if needed, include additional messages until (i) the trimmed conversation contains at least $10$k tokens and (ii) every tool call in the trimmed conversation has its corresponding tool output included as well. The summary is appended to the agent's instructions.

We place conversation to be summarized into the Summarizer's message history
and instruct it to view the message history as data. In this way, the Summarizer is able to observe images or PDF-files. If a previous conversation summary exists, it is appended to the Summarizer's instructions.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\appsubsection{Tools}

As some tools are shared between different agents, we list all available tools and indicate in Tab.~\ref{tab:agent_tools} which agent has access to which tool. The Orchestartor, the Plan-Updater and the Summarizer are omitted from this list as they do not have access to any tool.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{web\_search:} We use \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-web-search}{OpenAI's built-in web-search tool}.
    \item \textbf{bash(commands: str):} Executes the command string and captures the stdout and stderr output. If an output stream exceeds 40kB, it is forwarded to a file. If the command execution exceeds 10 minutes, both output streams are forwarded to separate files and the agent is given the PID of the process. This gives the agent the ability to work during long-lasting executions and to terminate a command if it is stuck.
    \item \textbf{wait(minutes: float):} Puts the agent to sleep. This tool is useful for long-lasting bash commands.
    \item \textbf{apply\_patch(operations: List[ApplyPatchOp]):} Applies patch operations to files. An patch operation is defined by its type ("create\_file", "update\_file", "delete\_file"), the file path and the V4A diff string (empty for deleting a file).\\
    Our tool is intentionally modeled after \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-apply-patch}{OpenAI's built-in apply\_patch tool}. This reference provides the relevant details behind the V4A diff string syntax.
    \item \textbf{read\_pdf(pdf\_file\_path: str):} Encodes a PDF file in base64 and wraps it in a file content block. The PDF file is then \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/pdf-files}{processed internally by OpenAI}.
    \item \textbf{read\_image(image\_file\_path: str):} Encodes an image in base64 and wraps it in an image content block. The image is then \href{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/images-vision}{processed internally by OpenAI}.
    \item \textbf{int-CLI-tools:} Collection of 4 tools used for an interactive CLI session.\\
    - int\_cli\_status() summarizes the state of the CLI. In particular, it indicates whether the agent has inspected the session before.\\
    - read\_int\_cli\_transcript(start\_line: int, end\_line: int) reads a slice of the CLI's transcript.\\
    - read\_int\_cli\_output(wait\_s: float) reads any new CLI output after waiting wait\_s seconds. If new output is received, it waits for 2 seconds and repeats until no additional output appears.\\
    - run\_int\_cli\_command(command: str, wait\_s: float): executes the command and reads the output with the read\_int\_cli\_output tool.
    \item \textbf{save\_answer(file\_path: str, content: str):} Saves the content to the file. If the file exists, it is overwritten.
    \item \textbf{list\_pdfs(search\_string: str):} Lists all PDF files matching the ".pdf"-appended search string by executing \lstinline|glob.glob(f"{search_string}.pdf")|.
\end{itemize}
